items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig in manufacturing?
items.correct: To guide a tool and ensure consistent, accurate repeated operations.
items.misconception: To hold the workpiece very tightly so it does not move.
items.why: Students confuse jigs with fixtures. A fixture HOLDS the workpiece; a jig GUIDES the tool (e.g., a drill jig guides the drill bit to the correct position) - though the terms are sometimes used loosely.

items.q: Which document sets out measurable criteria a product must meet?
items.why: It's Specification. “Brief” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Repeated cycles of refinement. “Single linear process” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many stages are typical in a basic design cycle (research, design, develop, prototype, test, evaluate)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
